39945102.title: Deepdive on Circuit Breaker pattern
39945102.transcript: Inside this lecture, let me give a quick introduction about what is the circuit breaker pattern and how it is going to help us in stopping the cascading failures inside our microservice network. Before I try to explain about the circuit breaker pattern inside microservices, first let me give a quick introduction about Circuit Breaker that we use as part of electrical system. We all use many circuit breakers inside our house or office. So what is the purpose of this circuit breaker? It is a safety device designed to protect the electrical circuit from excessive current or any potential fire hazards. Whenever a circuit breaker detects that there is a fault in the flow of the electricity may be due to the short circuit or overload of the electricity in such scenarios to ensure the safety and the stability of your house or the office, it is going to automatically open or it is going to automatically trip itself. So there won't be any electricity passing towards the components that you use inside your house or office. You can see here we also have one sample representation Since the circuit breaker is open now, my electric components like bulb, it is going to get protected because there is a short circuit or there is a overload detected by the circuit breaker. So with this, my bulb is not going to get affected. Think of a scenario if the circuit breaker is not there, the overloaded current at the short circuit is going to travel to the bulb inside my electric system and eventually my bulb is going to get damaged. The same concept is considered in building the circuit breaker pattern inside the software development. Let's try to understand how this is relevant to the circuit breaker pattern inside software development, just like inside electrical system, how the electricity is going to pass throughout the network. Very similarly, in a distributed environment, the calls to the remote resources and services is going to happen, and these remote calls may fail due to many reasons, maybe due to some transient faults or due to some slow network or maybe due to some timeout or maybe due to resources being overcommitted or maybe due to temporarily unavailable. The reason can be anything but. But most of the times these faults typically correct themselves after a short period of time. Maybe if one of the microservice is responding very slowly, it is not going to be in such state forever. At some point of time it is going to recover itself. Or if you take the network problem itself, the network problem is going to be a temporary problem. It is not going to be a permanent problem. So since these kind of issues are going to get resolved for after a short period of time, we need to be ready to face these kind of temporary issues inside our microservices network. So let's try to understand how the circuit breaker pattern is going to help in these kind of scenarios. The circuit breaker pattern, which is inspired from the electrical circuit breaker itself, it is going to monitor all the remote calls happening to a particular service. If a particular service like micro service, if it is taking too long to respond or if it is not responding or if it is having some network issues, the circuit breaker is going to kill that call. And at the same time, since it is monitoring all the calls that are going to the courts, microservice and if the majority of the calls are failing due to the slow response or due to some network issue, the circuit breaker implementation will pop up and it will make sure all the future requests coming to the courts microservice are failing immediately, which means it will never allow the traffic to the courts. Microservice Instead it is going to tell to the client applications my cards, microservice is not working. That's why I am sending the failure response immediately. With that, my dependent microservices like accounts microservice and gateway server. They don't have to wait for a longer time. So by failing fast we are preventing the ripple effect on the gateway server and the accounts microservice. With this approach, we are also making sure other rest APIs inside our microservices are not affected just because courts microservice is down. Maybe there are some microservices or rest APIs where only accounts and loans microservices they have to work together. All such remote calls will be successful because there is no ripple effect happened on my accounts. Microservice with the help of this circuit breaker pattern. So here you may have a very good question, which is I said my circuit breaker pattern is going to stop all the future requests coming towards the courts. Microservice And it is going to immediate failure response to the client microservices like accounts microservice will this going to be forever? Of course not. At some point of time your courts microservice is going to get recovered because your circuit breaker pattern stopped all the incoming. Traffic and gave enough time for your car's microservice to recover. That's why periodically the circuit breaker pattern also sends some partial traffic to the car's microservice to check if my car's microservice is recovered or not. If this partial traffic, which is allowed by the circuit breaker is successful, then my circuit breaker pattern is going to allow all the incoming requests towards the car's microservice. Whereas if this partial traffic, which it allowed previously is also failing, then again, it is going to stop all the incoming requests for few more seconds or few more minutes based upon our configurations. I'll show you this in demo inside our microservices. Then it is going to be super, super clear for you. So there are very good amount of advantages due to this circuit breaker pattern. The primary advantages are it is going to fail your request very fastly instead of your accounts. Microservice waiting for more than 10s and realize that my car's microservice is not working. It is going to fail the incoming request to the cards microservice instantly with that my accounts microservice never have to wait for 10s instead. Now it is going to get the error response within a one second or even within a second. And the next advantage that we have with the circuit breaker pattern is we can make our microservices fail gracefully. Maybe whenever my car's microservice is down or responding very slowly, I can write some fallback mechanism which will fail the request gracefully. I'll explain how to write a fallback mechanism with the help of Circuit Breaker in the coming lectures. And the last primary advantage that we have is the circuit breaker is going to help microservices to recover seamlessly because it is going to give them some rest for a short period of time, like 30s or 90s. Based upon your configurations within the 30s or 90s, your microservices will try to recover because there is no incoming traffic. And if there is some network problem within 30 or 90s, most of the time the network problems will also get resolved. So this is a very quick introduction about circuit breaker pattern. In the next lecture, let me try to explain you how this is all going to happen. Inside circuit breaker pattern with the help of a sample flow. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture by.

39945112.title: Three states of Circuit Breaker pattern
39945112.transcript: Inside this lecture, let me try to explain how circuit breaker pattern is going to control the traffic coming towards a particular microservice. By default circuit breaker pattern is not going to monitor all your microservices. We need to configure this circuit breaker pattern wherever we need. Think like you have configured this circuit breaker pattern for one of the microservice. Whenever we activate the circuit breaker pattern on any microservice, it is going to control the flow of traffic towards the microservice by using three different states. The very first state is closed state. Initially, when the application starts by default, your circuit breaker will be in this closed state. Inside this closed status, it is going to accept all the requests coming towards your particular microservice. You can also try to correlate this with the circuit breaker present inside the electric system. Inside the electric system, whenever the circuit is closed, that means all the current is going to be passed to the next components. Very similarly inside microservices, when the circuit breaker pattern is in the closed status, it means it is going to allow all the traffic towards a microservice. With this closed status, your circuit breaker pattern is going to monitor all the requests coming towards your microservices and it will try to understand whether the microservice is responding properly or if there are any network issue or there are any slow response from the microservice. So based upon these monitoring and analysis, if my circuit breaker identifies that most of the requests coming to the microservices are being failed, then it is going to immediately jump to the open status. How it is going to transition from close to open status is based upon the failure rate threshold that you have defined. Maybe you might have configured saying that if 50% of my traffic that is coming towards my microservice is failing, then please move to the open status. So based upon these configurations, if the circuit breaker identifies that 50% of the traffic is failed, then immediately it is going to jump from the close to the open status. Whenever the circuit breaker is in open status, that means it is not going to send any request to the actual microservice. Instead, it is going to fail immediately and send the error response to the invoking microservices at the client applications. This way it will make sure there is no ripple effect on the invoking microservices due to the issues in a particular microservice. And do you think this open status will be forever? Off course not. Keeping the circuit breaker open forever means we are never going to allow that traffic towards a particular microservice. So what behind the scenes will happen is my circuit breaker pattern will wait in the open status based upon your configurations. Maybe if you have configured for 90s up to 90s, the circuit breaker pattern will be in open status and it will give 90s time for my actual microservice to recover from the failures or from the network issues. After 90s, my circuit breaker pattern will move into the half open status. Inside this half open status the circuit breaker will allow only few requests based upon your configurations. Maybe it will allow 10 requests or 20 requests, and inside these ten are 20 requests. Again, if at least 50% of the requests are failing, then it will again jump back to the open status and it will wait again for 90s. So this cycle, from open to half open, half open to open will continuously happen periodically. At some point of time when my circuit breaker realizes that majority of the traffic or the requests are being processed successfully, then it will move from half open status to closed status. And with that my microservice, where I have configured, secured breaker pattern will continue to work normally. This sounds very complicated to achieve, but believe me, this is going to be super, super easy to configure secure breaker pattern inside microservices because we have friends like Resiliency4j and Springboard Framework. With the help of this libraries and frameworks, we can easily configure circuit breaker pattern. So let's try to implement this secure breaker pattern from the next lecture inside our microservices. But before coming to the next lecture, please make sure you are clear about the circuit breaker pattern and the three states that we have inside this pattern. Please try to rewatch the video or please refer to this slide. If if something is not clear. You can also read these information inside the official documentation of resiliency4j. . I hope this is clear. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39945166.title: Introduction to Retry pattern
39945166.transcript: In this lecture, let me try to introduce to you a new resiliency pattern, which is Retry Pattern. So what is a retry pattern? With the help of this pattern, we can configure multiple retry attempts whenever a service is temporarily failing. This pattern is going to be super, super helpful, especially in the scenarios like network disruption where the client requests may successful after a retry attempt. Let's try to understand more details about this pattern. Whenever we want to implement Retry Pattern inside our microservices, we need to be very clear on how many times we want to retry an operation. Whether you want to retry for three times or five times or ten times. So it is based upon your business logic. You need to decide on the number of retries. And this retry logic you can also conditionally invoke based upon many factors like error codes, exceptions or response status. And while we are retrying an operation, we can follow an backoff strategy. What is the purpose of this backup strategy? Let's try to understand the same. Without backup strategy, if you try to perform multiple retries, it is going to be a simple retry operation where every two seconds or every three seconds or one second you want to retry, which means there is a static time difference between multiple retry operations, whereas with the backup strategy, we can avoid overwhelming the system. So with the help of this strategy, we can gradually increase the delay between each retry known as exponential backoff. Maybe the first retry you are trying to do after two seconds and the second retry instead of retrying immediately after two seconds. This time you are going to retry after four seconds this way for each retry, you are going to wait for more time. And with this approach, there is a good chance that you may get a successful response and you are network issue might have resolved because you are giving enough time for the network issue to resolve. So that's why it is always recommended to use this backup strategy whenever we are trying to implement these retry pattern inside the microservices. And if needed, we can also integrate this retry pattern with the other patterns like circuit Breaker. By combining both retry pattern and the circuit breaker pattern, we can make our circuit breaker pattern to open after certain number of retries failed consecutively. So there is also a possibility where we can integrate or implement multiple resiliency patterns. I'm going to talk about implementing multiple resiliency patterns in the coming lectures, but for now, please note that if needed, we can also implement both retry and circuit breaker patterns inside our microservices. And the last important point that I want to highlight here is, whenever you are trying to implement retry pattern inside your microservices, please make sure you are implementing these retry pattern only for Idempotent operations. So what are Idempotent operations? So these are the operations which will not result in any side effect regardless of how many times you invoke them. For example, take the fetch API operation inside our microservices. If I try to retry multiple times, there is no harm is going to happen behind the scenes. I'm always going to get the response. But if you try to implement the same retry pattern for post operation or put operation, then there is a good chance you will have some side effects. Maybe you will create multiple records or maybe you are going to update the record multiple times, which will result into some data corruption or data side effects. That's why whenever you are trying to implement Retry Pattern, please make sure the respective API is of type Idempotent otherwise you are going to see serious side effects inside your microservices. So this is a very quick introduction about the retry pattern. From the next lecture, let's try to implement this retry pattern inside our microservices. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39945186.title: Introduction to Rate Limitter pattern
39945186.transcript: Have you ever played these balloon pop up game? During my childhood, my dad used to take me to the exhibition and I used to play this game a lot. The reason why I'm showing right now is this is very relevant to the new pattern that we are going to discuss. Let me explain you what is going to happen during this game. Whenever you try to play this game, the person who owns this balloon shop, he will give only limited set of resources like you will have only three chances or five chances based upon your payment. You are allowed to pop the balloons with the given set of resources. Beyond the resources that he has provided you are not allowed to play the game. Why the owner of this shop is following this rule. It is very simple because if you give unlimited chances to you, then he is going to be at loss. That's why he is going to limit your usage based upon your payment. Now let's go to the new pattern that we are going to discuss and you will realize how this is going to be related to the new pattern that we are going to discuss. The new pattern that we are going to discuss is rate limiter pattern. So what is this rate limiter pattern? Using this pattern inside microservices, we can control and limit the rate of incoming requests to a specific API or to a microservice. This pattern is majorly used to prevent abuse of the system and to protect the system resources and ensure there is a fair usage of the services by everyone. So let's try to understand more details about this pattern. In a microservices, multiple services will be deployed and these services may interact with each other to send a response to the client applications. However, sometimes if you don't put restrictions and controls on how many requests being consumed by a specific client or a specific person, then there can be a good chance the performance degradation or resource exhaustion may happen. And in some scenarios there is also a chance DoS attacks may happen. So what is a DoS attack? A single malicious user or a hacker is trying to send a continuous request to you maybe millions of requests to your server so that he can bring down your microservices and make your microservices network slow. So to avoid all these kind of scenarios and make sure everyone has their fair usage of service, we need to implement the rate limiter pattern so that we can enforce limits on the rate of incoming requests. If you are expecting 10,000 requests per second. Accordingly, you need to set up the infrastructure and accordingly you can implement rate limiter pattern as well. But all of a sudden if you are expecting some 1 million requests, then there is a chance that someone is trying to abuse your system or someone is trying to bring down your system. So to overcome these challenges we need to use rate limiter pattern. So what are the advantages when we implement these rate limiter pattern like we discussed, this will help your microservices from being overwhelmed by excessive or malicious requests from the hackers. It also ensures the stability, performance and availability of the services while providing controlled access to the resources inside the microservice. Basically, you are going to create a healthy environment where everyone can use the services as long as they are within the rate limits that we have configured. If we configure this rate limiter pattern inside our microservices, how it is going to stop the excess request is it is simply going to return an for 429 Http status code, which is too many requests and it cannot accept more requests. And this will indicates to clients that they can try to invoke the service after few seconds or few minutes. We can enforce this limitation based upon various strategies, like maybe we can try to limit the requests based upon the session, based upon an IP address or based upon the logged in user or based upon a tenant or based upon a server. So there can be many strategies that you can use. Additionally, we can also use this rate limiter pattern to provide services to users based upon their subscription tiers. For instance, inside your system you may have users like basic user, premier user and enterprise users. So for different, different types of users, you want to enforce different, different type of rate limits. We can implement these kind of requirements also with the help of rate limiter pattern. So this is a very quick introduction about this pattern and I'm assuming you are able to correlate with the balloon pop up game that I have shown previously. I'm right now having nostalgic feeling because with this balloon pop up game, a lot of my childhood memories came into my mind. I'm assuming the same case with you. Enjoy your nostalgic feeling. Thank you. And I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

That means a single user is trying to send a lot of traffic and with that, all other users are getting impacted. There is also an example where an user is sending high volume of requests for some analysis of the data and this is affecting some critical transactions. And other scenario is something in your system has gone wrong internally and as a result, you can't serve all of your regular traffic and need to drop low priority request. So these are all the scenarios where rate limiting is a good option. If you are interested, please read this blog. This is going to be super interesting. Now, if I go to the Spring Cloud Gateway here they're saying the RateLimiter pattern based upon the Redis is inspired, based upon the work done at the stripe. And in order to implement this, we need to add this dependency inside the pom.xml. And this is going to use the algorithm which going to work based upon the token bucket. To this token bucket algorithm. We need to pass three different type of properties. ReplenishRate, burst capacity and requested tokens. So what is replenishRate? This property defines how many requests per second to allow. In other words, this is the rate at which the token bucket is filled for this replenishRate. If you define 100, that means for every one second behind the scenes, 100 tokens will be added to your bucket. So if you wait for two seconds and if you are not consuming anything, then your bucket will have 200 requests and every bucket is assigned to a user or any other criteria based upon how you have defined KeyResolver. Now coming to the burst capacity with the help of replenishRate, we will be keep adding the number of requests per second. For example, if my replenishRate is 100 per second after 10s, if my end user is not using anything within this 10s, the bucket is going to have 1000 requests and if you keep filling it. At some point of time, he will have millions of requests inside his bucket. So to avoid this kind of overfilling the bucket with a large number of values, we are going to set the burst capacity. Using this property we are going to set what is the number of tokens that bucket can hold. So if I set 200 as the burst capacity after two seconds, my replenishRate is going to fill 200 tokens. And from the third second, if my end user is not using any of the tokens present inside his bucket, then since the bucket is already full, the replenishRate cannot add more number of tokens. And the third property that we have here is requested tokens. So using this property we are going to define how many tokens a request cost. Usually by default each request will cost a single token, but if needed, you can change this to 10 or 100 accordingly, the number of tokens will be consumed in the bucket for each request. Here we also have some examples like a steady rate is accomplished by setting the same value in replenishRate and burst capacity. So if you mention replenishRate and burst capacity as 100, that means every second inside my user bucket we are going to add 100 tokens. And if he's not using them, they will get wasted and the tokens inside the bucket will be deleted and replaced with the new tokens every one second. But in case if you want to allow temporary burst, maybe if you want to give some flexibility to your end user since you have not used the 100 tokens in the previous second and in the current second, you can use all of them in such scenarios you can mention burst capacity higher than replenishRate. For example, if you keep burst capacity as 200 and a replenishRate as 100, then that means in the first second, even though the end user is not able to use his tokens in the second second, he should be able to use all his 200 tokens at a time. So that's how we need to handle the temporary burst. There is also an example. Suppose if you have a requirement that you want to allow only one request per minute. So for these kind of scenarios, you can see they have given an example where replenishRate you have to mention as one, which means for every one second you are going to add one token into the bucket so that by the end of 60s or one minute, the replenishRate is going to add total 60 tokens into the bucket and the burst capacity we need to set as 60. That means the maximum number of tokens that we can add to the bucket is going to be 60 at any point of time. The bucket will have only maximum 60 tokens. And after the burst capacity we need to mention requested token is equal to 60. That means whenever he make a single request, 60 tokens will be consumed. With this we are making him to be able to send only one request per minute. So accordingly, based upon your requirements, you can play with these properties. Now if you can scroll down. So this is how you can activate the rate limiter and configure these three properties like replenishRate burst capacity and request tokens. So here they have given the example with the help of Yaml configurations. But in the next lecture I'm going to show you how to achieve this with the help of Java based configurations. And if you can scroll down, they also gave an example on how to create a bean. So you can see from the bean they are trying to return the user details. So inside the request they are some query params inside the query params there is a query param with the name user, so they are trying to get the value present inside the query param with the name user. So this is how they are trying to provide some KeyResolver, but it is up to us based upon our business requirements. We can write any kind of logic inside this KeyResolver which is going to act as a basic criteria to enforce all these properties. If you can scroll up here we have an interesting information, like if you mention the burst capacity value to zero, that means it is going to block all the requests. So please make sure you are not mentioning the value as zero. Otherwise your end user will not be able to send any request towards your microservices. I hope you are clear about this quick introduction. In the next lecture, let's try to implement this inside our GatewayServer. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39945198.title: Introduction to Bulkhead pattern
39945198.transcript: Inside this lecture, let's try to discuss about a new pattern, which is Bulkhead pattern. So what is this Bulkhead pattern? With the help of this bulkhead pattern, we can improve the resilience and isolation of components or services within a microservice network or within a system. You might be wondering why there is an image of boat or ship on the slide. The reason why we have this image is this bulkhead pattern inspired from the concept of bulkheads in the ships. So what are these bulkheads? If you can see the image of shape using these bulkheads, we are physically partitioning the entire ship so that even if one of the compartment is flooded with the water, it is not going to affect other partitions or the compartments inside the ship. So this will enhance the overall stability and safety of the ship or vessel. Did you watch Titanic movie? I think these are very dumb question. I think everyone on the earth saw the movie of Titanic. So coming to the movie, when the ship is collided with the mountain in the middle of the sea, the ship did not immediately merged into the ocean. It took a lot of time. There is a lot of story happened from the collision to the climax. The water did not enter into the entire ship at a time because behind the scenes they are bulkheads in the ship. When you lock a compartment, the water is not going to enter into other compartments very easily using the same bulkhead inside the ships. The bulkhead pattern is inspired, which we are trying to discuss from the software perspective. So with the help of this pattern, we can isolate and limit the impact of failures or high loads in one component from spreading to the other component. It also help to ensure that a heavy load in one part of the system does not, bringing down the entire system. And that enables other components to work to continue functioning independently. I'm going to share more details around this in few seconds. I know this is super complex to understand. Please bear with me a few seconds. I'm going to show you a diagram then it is going to make more sense to you. How a bulkhead pattern is going to achieve the stability is, with the help of this pattern, we can allocate or assign the resources to a specific REST API or to a specific microservice. So that the excessive usage of resources we can avoid. On a high level the summary is, with the help of bulkhead pattern, we can define the resource boundaries for all the components inside a microservice and this will enhance the resiliency and stability of the system. So let's try to understand this bulkhead pattern with the help of a diagram. So first you can see without the bulkhead pattern, this is the accounts microservice and think like inside accounts microservice I have two REST APIs. One is myAccount and the other one is myCustomerDetails. And not many clients they are trying to send the request to these two REST APIs. We know that my account REST API is a simple REST API because it does not depend on other microservice, whereas myCustomerDetails REST API is a REST API that depends on other microservices like loans and cards. So the amount of processing time or the amount of business logic complexity is going to be higher inside the myCustomerDetails compared to the myAccount. Without bulkhead, what will happen myCustomerDetails API is going to eat all the thread, all the resources available inside a Docker container. So this is going to affect the performance of myAccount. So at some point of time myAccount is not going to get enough threads or resources to process the incoming requests from the clients. So with the help of bulkhead pattern, let's try to understand how the picture is going to change. Like you can see here, with the help of bulkhead patterns, we are defining the boundaries for these REST APIs. So inside these boundaries we are going to assign for myAccount or for myCustomerDetails. These are the threads or these are the resources that it can use from the thread pool. With this clear boundaries now myAccount is going to work happily, regardless of how many requests that myCustomerDetails is receiving because we have provided minimum number of threads from the thread pool to the myAccount API as well with the help of bulkhead pattern. So let's try to understand how to implement these bulkhead pattern by looking at the official documentation inside the Spring Cloud gateway there is no support for the bulkhead pattern as of now. With that, we can only implement bulkhead pattern with the help of resiliency4j library. Here if you can scroll on this page. There is an annotation which you can use for bulkhead. You can see with the help of @Bulkhead annotation, we can configure the bulkhead related configurations and to this type we can mention Threadpool. That means with these bulkhead configurations we are trying to assign the thread pools to this operation or to this method. So the properties related to bulkhead you can identify if you can scroll up on this page. So let's scroll up, up, up. So here you can see there are properties which are related to bulkhead where you can mention what are the maximum concurrent calls that a particular bulkhead pattern can support on top of a REST API. And similarly we have max weight duration. So this is normal bulkhead configuration where you can only control the concurrent calls. But if you want to control the threads also, then with the help of this thread pool configurations, you can assign maximum thread pool size. What is the core thread pool size? What is the queue capacity? So these are all the properties that you can use to define the bulkhead configurations. I'm not going to implement these inside our microservices and I'm not going to show you the demo of bulkhead. The reason is to see that demo of this pattern we need some commercial tools or some performance tools like Loadrunner or Jmeter, where we can see the thread usage. So that is super complicated process. That's why I'm not going to show you that in the demo, but I'm assuming you are clear in future whenever you have these kind of scenarios where you want to define the boundaries for your APIs inside the microservice, then please leverage this and with the help of your performance testing team, you can try to validate your changes. I hope you are clear about the bulkhead pattern. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39945204.title: Aspect order of Resiliency patterns
39945204.transcript: As of now, inside this course, we have discussed various resiliency patterns supported by this Resilience4j library. And inside the course we have implemented all these patterns inside our microservices individually. But sometimes you may have some complex business logic where you may end up combining various resiliency patterns. In such scenarios, you may have a question like what is the order that my resiliency library is going to follow? If I have multiple patterns defined for a single API or for a single method or for a single service. So let's try to understand the same for the same inside the official documentation of resiliency4j. If you can click on this Getting Started under Spring Boot two and three, you will have this page. If you can scroll down on this page, there is an information related to the aspect order. So let me zoom this page. Here you can see by default, this is the order that resiliency4j library is going to follow. So Retry is going to be applied at the end. So first there'll be bulkhead after the bulkhead TimeLimiter and after the time limiter we have RateLimiter and after the RateLimiter we have circuit breaker and after the circuit breaker at the end we have retry. Sometimes you may be fine with these default order, but if you have some complex scenario where you want to change this default order, then it is going to be super, super easy with the help of these properties. So you can see using these properties, you can define the order of execution inside your Yaml configurations or inside your application.yml. So with these configurations we are giving higher priority to the retry. That means the higher priority means it is going to have higher value and that is going to be executed first and post that only circuit breaker is going to be executed. So that's what it is confirming. Circuit breaker starts after the retry finish its work. So this is a quick information that I want to give you, but my humble advice here is please don't try to use all these patterns that will be like doing overengineering inside your microservices. And without proper testing, you may get some surprises inside the production. Please do the due diligence and post that only go with the required patterns inside your microservices. 39945226.title: Observability vs. Monitoring
39945226.transcript: Inside this lecture, let's try to talk in detail about observability and monitoring. First, let's try to understand what is observability. Observability is the ability to understand the internal state of a system by observing or by understanding its output. For example, in the context of microservices, observability can be achieved by collecting and analyzing data from a variety of sources such as metrics, logs and traces. Using all these information, we can try to understand how our microservices that are running internally, how well a particular microservice is processing all the incoming requests or how many errors my microservice is throwing. So all such internal information we can try to understand with the concept of observability. There are three pillars for observability. The very first one is metrics. Metrics are quantitative measurements of the health of a system. They can be used to track things like CPU usage, memory usage and response times. So using these metrics, we can measure our microservices performance or health. The next pillar that we have is logs. So what are logs? Logs are a record of events that occur inside a system. We can put log statements inside our method calls, so whenever that particular method is being executed, my logs will be generated indicating that a particular record of event is executed successfully. So using these logs we can track things like errors, exceptions and other unexpected events. We all know how important logs are while debugging an issue without logs, it is impossible to identify an issue inside the production environment. After logs the last pillar of observability is traces. So what are traces? Traces are a record of the path that a request takes through a system inside our microservices network. They can be hundreds of microservices. A particular request may travel through the multiple microservices. So to understand what is a path that my request travelled inside the microservice network, we can use that traces. Using these traces information we can track the performance of a request at each microservice or at each method. And with that we should be able to easily identify any performance bottlenecks. By collecting the data from these three pillars, we can analyze the data and gain a good understanding of the internal state of our microservices. This understanding can help developers to identify and troubleshoot problems, and in some times we can also use this information to identify the bottlenecks and improve the performance. And apart from performance and troubleshooting problems, with the help of these data, we can also ensure the overall health of the system. I hope you are clear what is observability. Now let's try to understand what is monitoring. Monitoring in microservice context involves checking the telemetry data available for the application and defining the alerts for known failure state. We saw inside the observability we use the data like logs, metrics and traces for troubleshooting any defects or performance issues. But with the help of monitoring, we can build some dashboards and build alerts and notifications based upon metrics, logs and traces information. Maybe we can try to build a dashboard which can be used by the operations team to monitor the overall health of our microservices. If the CPU utilization of a particular container or a microservice crossed more than 80%, then I want a alert to be triggered. So all such scenarios we can achieve with the help of monitoring. Monitoring is very important inside microservices because it will allow us to identify and troubleshoot problems. If you have hundreds of microservices running in different containers, in different virtual machines monitoring all of them, 24 by seven is going to be super, super impossible task. That's why by collecting and analyzing the data from the individual microservices, we can identify problems before they cause any outages or other disruptions. For example, if you take the same CPU usage example if a particular microservice CPU usage is more than 80%, then I can try to add one more instance of the microservice so that the traffic will be divided between these two instances. So here, using this monitoring information, I'm trying to avoid the outage by adding more number of instances. And apart from identifying and troubleshooting problems using monitoring, we can also track the health of our microservices. Using dashboards, alerts and notifications we can easily understand which microservice is underperforming or which microservice is having some network problems or any other kind of problems that will affect the performance of my microservice. And at last, using monitoring, we can try to optimize our microservices by onboarding more number of instances or by killing the microservice, which is having problems. And in the same place we can try to onboard a new container or a new microservice instance. With all these approaches, we are going to improve the performance and reliability of the microservices. So here you may have a question like both observability and monitoring they are looking very similar and you may also have confusion like what is the difference between them? Let me try to answer the same. Monitoring and observability can be considered as a two sides of the same coin. Both rely on the same types of telemetry data to enable insights about your microservices. These telemetry data or data types, includes metrics, traces, and logs using these kind of information only both observability and monitoring are going to work. Let's try to understand in detail what is the difference between monitoring and observability. For the same, if you try to take an example of iceberg, whatever you are able to see on the top of the iceberg, we can call that as monitoring because we will be easily able to see how our microservices health, what is the CPU usage, how many threads are being used? All such information we can easily see with the help of monitoring because inside the monitoring we are going to build the dashboards, alerts and notifications. But whatever information which you cannot easily see, all such information, we call it as observability, there might be some NullPointerException inside my microservice network until unless I go and look into the logs, I cannot really understand where the issue is. So such kind of information comes under the observability. Let me give you a table which has differences between monitoring and observability. Like you can see here, if you take the purpose of monitoring and observability, monitoring is going to help you to identify and troubleshoot problems. Whereas with the help of observability, we can try to understand the internal state of a system. Coming to that what kind of data that will be used inside the monitoring is metrics, traces and logs inside observability. Apart from these metrics, trace and logs, other kind of information also will be leveraged. Moving on to the next information, which is goal. The goal of the monitoring is to identify the problems, whereas the observability goal is to understand how a system works. Now let's talk about the approach. Inside the monitoring the approach is going to be reactive. So what is reactive approach? When I say reactive, we are going to react when a problem occurs. For example, your operations team can react when a particular microservice is facing some network problems or some performance issues. Without seeing those events. Your operations team cannot react to them. That's why inside the monitoring the approach will always be reactive, whereas inside the observability the approach is going to be proactive. For example, your microservice might be throwing some NullPointerExceptions. Maybe it is not throwing for each and every request for some random scenario or for some rare scenario. A particular microservice is throwing a RuntimeException or NullPointerException. So as a developer you will try to proactively identify the issues so that you can provide a fix in the future release. So this is a proactive approach. I hope you are clear what is monitoring and observability. If you ask me to explain the difference of monitoring and observability in simple words, I would say monitoring is about collecting data, and observability is about understanding data. We can also say monitoring is reacting to the problems while observability is fixing them in real time. Like any runtime exceptions or performance issues, we have to deep dive into the information to understand the internal state and fix them in real time. With this, I am assuming you are clear about what is observable and monitoring. Always, please note that observability will help you to understand the internal state of a system, whereas monitoring will help you to identify and troubleshoot problems with the help of alerts, dashboards, notifications. 39945300.title: Introduction to Distributed Tracing in microservices
39945300.transcript: As of now, we discussed about the two pillars of observability and monitoring, which are logs and metrics. Now, from this lecture, let's try to focus on the third pillar of observability and monitoring, which is tracing. With the two pillars that we have discussed which are related to event logs and metrics. We can only derive the internal condition of an application or overall health of an application. So the information that we get with the help of event logs, health probes and metrics, they fail to help developers in debugging the issues, especially inside the distributed environment like microservices or cloud native applications. Because inside the distributed applications like microservices, a user request can travel multiple applications or multiple microservices. And with these kind of complexity, we should have some mechanism for the developers to understand up to which microservice are up to which application, a request is travelled and how much time it is taking at each service so that the developer can pinpoint the exact location where the issue is happening inside distributed applications. So to handle these challenge or to handle these problem, we have a concept which is distributed tracing. So this is a technique used especially inside microservices or cloud native applications to understand and analyze the flow of requests as they propagate across multiple services and the components inside the distributed environment. So with these tracing information, the developers can diagnose any kind of issues inside any kind of complex and distributed systems. In real projects, there will be hundreds of microservices deployed if a request has to travel more than ten microservices to get a successful response, the developer should have a clear information how the request is traveling under each microservice, which method is invoked, how much time it is taking at each method. So until unless the developer has this distributed tracing information, he cannot debug issue inside the distributed systems. So here you may have a question like I understand the concept of distributed tracing, but how to implement this distributed tracing? One of the best or the possible solution to implement this distributed tracing is, we should generate a unique identifier known as correlationId, which is going to generate for each request at the entry point of your distributed system or microservices. Previously we discussed a scenario inside Gateway server where at the entry point we try to generate a correlationId and the same correlationId we send to the accounts, loans and cards, microservice. And with the correlationId, we are able to easily track the request that is traveling from gateway server to accounts, accounts to cards and loans microservice. The same correlationId can be used as a perfect solution for distributed tracing. Because with the help of correlationId, we can track our microservice request where it is traveling and up to which point it travel. What are the exceptions it throwed So all such information we should be able to understand with the help of these correlationId implementation. But do you think generating the correlationId and attaching the same for all the logs inside our microservices network is a feasible option for the developers? Off course not, because the developer has to visit each log present inside the microservice and he need to make sure he's appending the correlationId that is generated by the gateway server. So this is going to be a super, super complex or challenging task. That's why we are going to look for a better option compared to what we have discussed previously with the help of Gateway Server to generate this correlationId using which we can try to implement distributed tracing inside our microservices. before we try to understand what are the best practices that we have around this. First, let me share some details. What are the standards that we need to follow while generating the distributed tracing details. Like you can see here, distributed tracing always recommends to follow three important components whenever we are trying to implement distributed tracing inside our microservices. The very first component is the tags. Using these tags, we can build a metadata that offer details like username of the authenticated user or the microservice identifier. So if you attach these metadata information to the logs, you can easily identify from the log statement to which microservice are to which metadata information that a particular log belongs. For example, if you attach the application name for all your logs like accounts, cards and loans, then it is going to be super, super easy for you to which microservice application a particular distributed tracing logging belongs. When we try to implement these and see a demo, then it is going to be more clear for you. Apart rfom this tag we should also have a trace ID. A trace ID has to be generated at the starting of your request. Maybe when a request is trying to enter into your microservice network at the Edge server. The trace ID has to be generated. The same trace ID has to be attached to the all the logs that are associated to that request, regardless of where the request is traveling inside your microservice network throughout all the microservices where a particular request is being processed. The same trace ID has to be present inside the logs. And the third component that we have is span ID. What is this span ID? A span represents each individual stage of request processing. For example, your request may travel accounts, loans and cards microservice. under each service it should have its own span ID because inside accounts microservice you may invoke multiple methods and similarly inside other microservices also you are going to implement a lot many methods. That's why we should assign a ID value which is specific to each microservice. So for all the logs that are generated within the accounts microservice, it is going to have the same span ID along with the trace ID that is common for all the microservices. I know this is super confusing for you. Let me show you a sample demo of this. For the same let me go to the next slide. 39945446.title: What is OpenID Connect & why it is important
39945446.transcript: Inside this lecture, I'm going to talk about what is OpenID Connect and what is its relation with the OAuth2 framework. Many times I see developers claiming that please don't use Oauth2, please use OpenID Connect because that is a better framework or it is a replacement of the Oauth2 framework. I don't want you to be such a developer. That's why I'm going to spend few minutes explaining you, what is this OpenID Connect and and how it is related to the Oauth2 framework. We discussed about the Oauth2 framework previously. The primary reason why the OAuth two specification built is, to support the authorization but not the authentication. So there is a clear difference between authentication and authorization. With the help of authentication, we can only authenticate the end user whether he is a legitimate user by validating his credentials. Once we authenticate the end user, he can access the secured pages inside an application. Instead of making him to access any kind of functionality and perform any kind of action, we can enforce privileges or role based access mechanism with the help of authorization. So authorization comes only after authentication. So the OAuth2 framework built to support the authorization. So that with the help of these authorization, the applications can provide temporary access or limited access to the third party applications. But since two framework became super, super famous, a lot many organizations, they found a way to use the OAuth2 framework for authentication as well. If you can recall the Stack overflow scenario, I don't have an account inside the stack Overflow. Stack Overflow is trying to authenticate me with the help of GitHub credentials and GitHub does not know what Stack overflow is doing with my access token. GitHub will be under the assumption that Stack Overflow is requesting the email details of the resource owner and resource owner also fine providing these privileges to the stack overflow. That's why it is going to issue an access token that will allow the stack overflow to read my email details. But behind the scenes the stack overflow is also authenticating me inside the Stack Overflow website and it is creating an account of mine with the help of the email address received from the GitHub server. Since organizations found a loophole on using the OAuth2 framework both for authentication and authorization, there is a need came for the OpenID Connect. So let's try to understand what is this OpenID Connect. OpenID Connect is a protocol that developed or sits on top of the Oauth2 framework, while Oauth2 provides an authorization via an access token containing Scopes information. This OpenID Connect framework, which built on top of the OAuth2. It provides authentication by introducing a new ID token, and these ID token contains a set of information about the end user or about the resource owner. Like I said before, organizations, they started using Oauth2 framework both for authentication and authorization and OAuth2 framework It is going to issue an access token. And with this access token, the client applications, they can only access the protected resources that are present behind the resource server. And this access token is not giving any clue about who is the end user, what are his details. Since the client applications does not have a concrete information about the resource owner or end user. Lot many organizations they used to share the details about the end user inside this access token only by sending his email details or by sending his mobile number. Everyone they used to follow their own style of sharing the end user details inside the access token itself or using some other approach. So since there is no standard, everyone in the industry felt that we need some better approach to know the end user or resource owner details inside the OAuth2 framework. That's why they built a small wrapper on top of OAuth2, which is OpenID. So whenever we use OpenID along with the OAuth2, we're also going to get an extra token with the name ID token and with this ID token and OpenID connect, the industry now has a standards around sharing the identity details among the applications. When we implement these OAuth2 and OpenID Connect Inside our Microservices, I'm going to show you the access token ID token that are issued by the server. So don't worry about how these tokens are going to look like. When we see the demo, it is going to be super clear for you. I know you may still have some confusion. Let me try to explain this with the help of visual diagram. Like you can see here, we have Http protocol. So Http protocol is a base protocol for all the communication inside the web. On top of these Http protocol oauth2 framework is built and this OAuth2 framework is going to support authorization. On top of this over to framework, there is a small wrapper framework built which is OpenID Connect, and this OpenID Connect is going to support authentication. In simple words, we can say OpenID Connect Flow looks the same as Oauth2. Whenever we are using OpenID Connect, we need to send a scope which is OpenID and behind the scenes the Auth server by looking at this OpenID scope, it is going to issue two types of token. One is access token and ID token. If you don't send the scope OpenID, you're going to simply receive the access token. Let's try to understand more details about OpenID Connect. Like you can see under this section, which is why is OpenID Connect? I have mentioned all the details, like why there is a need for this OpenID Connect when we already have over two framework. I mentioned all the story that I have discussed since everyone inside the industry, they are looking for a standard to share the details about the end user or the resource owner, there is a need came to develop these OpenID Connect on top of the Oauth2 framework. So you can also see there is a Venn diagram here. On the right hand side we have OAuth2 framework which is going to support the authorization, and with these authorization we are going to control the access management with the help of scope. Whereas on the left hand side we have other component which is OpenID Connect, which is going to support the authentication. And with this OpenID Connect, we can easily identify the identity details of the end user. So by combining both of these two standards, a new concept came into picture, which is IAM Identity and Access Management. First, any auth server it has to identify the end user and post that only it should provide the access to the end user. The short form of OpenID Connect is OIDC with the help of these oidc now inside the industry there is a standardized approach to know the details of the end user. So whenever an organization implements OIDC along with the Oauth2, they are going to get three benefits. The very first one is, there is a standardized way to know the end user details or resource owner details with the help of the scopes like OpenID, profile, email and address. Because all these details are related to the end user only like his profile details, email details or address details. So whenever a client application looking for profile information of the end user, with the help of this OIDC, they can request for a scope profile. And similarly we have other scopes like OpenID email and address. And the second advantage is, the ID token that is going to get generated by the OpenID standards. It is going to follow the same standards like Access Token, both these access token and ID token. They are going to use the JWT standard, which is Json web token standard. And the last advantage is this OpenID Connect exposes a standardized endpoint with the name /userifo. . Anytime a client application wants to know the details about the logged in user or the resource owner, they can simply invoke this user info endpoint supported by the OpenID. So with this I'm assuming your super, super clear about what is difference between Oauth2 and OpenID. So please never say that Oauth2 is a replacement of OpenID without Oauth2 there is no OpenID. And also please don't say that OpenID is better than OAuth2 framework. We should never say because without Oauth2 there is no OpenID. But instead of that we can say that by combining these OpenID and Oauth2, we can provide capabilities of identity and access management or in short form IAM to the client applications. I hope this is clear. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

Many companies they saw an opportunity building products around the specifications provided by the Oauth2 and OpenID. So let's try to explore a few of them. Like you can see here, these are website of KEYCLOCK. Keyclock is an open source identity and access management product. With the help of these keyclock product, we can easily set up authorization server for an organization. Very big organizations like Google, GitHub, Facebook, Twitter, so all these companies they can afford to build their own authorization server based upon the standards defined in the Oauth2 and OpenID. But how about the smaller organizations or organizations who are not interested to build these auth server from scratch? So throughout this course we are going to use these key clock products only because this is open source. And we also have fusion auth, Forgerock. So these are all the products. For some reason, if your organization is looking to build their own authorization server to make their journey easy, recently Spring team also they developed a new project with the name Spring Authorization Server. Using this Spring authorization server, anyone can build their own authorization server, but please note that this is a very new project inside the spring ecosystem and I see it has to mature a lot compared to other products like Keycloak, Okta, Cognito. 39945452.title: Deep dive of Client Credentials grant type flow
39945452.transcript: Now is the time to deep dive into the Oauth2 framework. Just by knowing the definition of Oauth2 and OpenID Connect. You cannot implement security inside our microservices network. We need to deep dive into these standards and learn the applicable grant type flows for our microservices network. Inside this lecture, we are going to discuss about client credentials grant type flow available inside the Oauth2 framework. Like I said before, Oauth2 has various grant type flows that we can use in implementing security inside our web applications based upon the type of communication. So before I try to explain more details about these client credentials grant type flow, first let me give clarification under which scenarios we need to use this grant type flow. We need to use this grant type flow. When two APIs are when two different backend servers, they are trying to communicate with each other. So in this communication, if you want to enforce security, then this is the perfect grant flow that we can use. So here there will be three actors or three roles or three jargons will be involved inside this flow. The very first and important component is auth server. This arch server is responsible to authenticate the client application and issue the access token. So how we are going to build this auth server like we discussed, we are going to leverage Keycloak to set up the auth server inside our Microservice network. Now coming to the resource server, so what is this resource server? We'll make our gateway server as a resource server so that anyone who wants to communicate with our gateway server, which is acting as a edge server for our microservice, they need to make sure they are authenticated with the ad server and post authentication with auth server they should get an access token from the auth server and the same it has to be sent to the resource server. The resource server are the gateway server is going to send the response only if it receives the proper access token from the client application that is issued by the auth server. With that quick introduction, let me show you a sample flow how these client credentials grant type flow is going to work. In the very first step, the client application is going to ask auth server that I want to access a protected resource available inside the resource server, but my resource server is not going to respond me because it expects access token from me. That's why please issue me an access token. So the client is going to request the auth server to issue an access token and in the same process the client application should prove its identity. For the same, it is going to send the client credentials to the auth server saying that this is my client ID and this is my client secret. And here please note that the client application is some other back end application or some other API or some other external microservice, who is trying to invoke our resource server. So that's why it is also going to highlight there is no end user involved, there is no UI application involved. That's what it is trying to convey in the very first step. Once my auth server receives the request and once it validated the credentials provided by the client application, it is going to issue an access token to the client application. Once the access token is received by the client application, the client application is going to send the same access token to the resource server. Along with the access token it will also convey which resource it is trying to access. Now my resource server will take that access token and behind the scenes it will validate the access token with the auth server and if the client application has enough privileges to invoke that particular secure API or secure resource, then my resource server is going to respond to the client application. Your token is validated successfully. Here are the protected resources that you have requested, which means inside the step four, my resource server is going to send the response back to the client based upon the request that it sent. So in our scenario, the resource server is going to be gateway. So behind the scenes, if my client application is trying to create an account, the gate server will validate the access token and once the access token is validated and once it feels like the client application has enough privileges to create an account, then behind the scenes it is going to invoke other microservices that are available inside the microservice network like accounts Microservice. Once it receives the successful response from the accounts microservice the same response it is going to forward to the client application. I tried to mention more details about this flow in the next slide. Let me go to that. Like you can see here in the step one, whenever the client is making a request to the auth server for an access token, the client application has to provide what is a clientID, what is the client secret. So these are the credentials of the client application which it might have received during the registration process with the auth server and inside the same request. It should also mention what are the scope details that it is requesting. So these scopes, like we discussed, these are very similar to authorities. If my client application is looking for email details, then the scope is going to be email. So based upon my client application requirements, it is going to mention the corresponding scope inside the request. And this doesn't mean that whatever my client application is asking, the auth server is going to construct that scope. It is not going to work that way. While registering these clients with the auth server, the admin of the ad server like Keycloak server, they will make some configuration for this clientId I want to only allow this set of scopes. So when my client application is sending the scope, it has to make sure whatever it is sending inside the request, it has to match with the list of scopes inside the auth server. If it is trying to be smart and try to get some other scope which is not defined inside the auth server, then it is going to get an error as a response. So that's the details around scope. And apart from these clientId, client secret and scope inside the request, the client application should also send grant type. Inside this grant type it is going to mention the value as client credentials. With this we are telling to the auth server. The current grant flow that it needs to follow is client credentials grant type flow. So since there won't be any end user involved in this scenario, the ad server will not ask credentials of end user or resource owner. It will simply assume there is no resource owner inside this flow. So this is the most simplest grant type flow In Oauth2 we are going to discuss most complex grant flow also in the coming lectures. And the last important point, which is like I said, we need to use this authentication flow or grant type flow only if there is no end user and there is no UI application involved. This grant flow is strictly for the scenarios where two different applications are trying to communicate with each other using backend APIs. I hope you are clear. If something is not clear, don't worry, I'm going to explain the same grant type flow by taking our Easy Bank Microservices as an example. And at the same time, when we implement this grant type flow and see the demo inside our microservices network, then it is going to be super clear for you. But for now, please note that we should use this grant type flow only in the scenarios. When two backend APIs are two backend servers or two applications are trying to communicate with each other. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39945454.title: Securing Gateway server using Client Credentials grant type flow - Theory
39945454.transcript: In the previous lecture, we discussed very quickly about client credentials grant type flow available inside the Oauth2 framework. Now using the same grant type flow, I'm going to explain you how we are going to secure our Gateway server, which is acting as an edge server for our microservices. Like you can see here, so first we are going to have some client application so this client application can be some external service or some external API or some external backend server. So they are trying to communicate with our microservice network. So this is the client application. As a next step, we are going to have the auth server which we are going to set up with the help of key clock inside the Easy Bank microservices network. In the very first step, like you can see, the client is going to connect with the auth server, with the client credentials and get a access token. So whatever external service or external API client who want to connect with my microservices behind the scenes, they need to work with the admins of the key clock to register themselves as a valid client application. Because the keyclock should not give access tokens for all type of external clients. Only approved external client applications by my key clock admin or by my business leaders only those applications should be able to communicate with my auth server and with the gateway server. So think like this client application is a valid client, so the client credentials are valid as a response to this request inside the step one, the keyclock is going to issue an access token. Using the same access token the client application is going to invoke a path available inside the edge server along with the access token issued by the auth server. So here you can see the Edge server or the gateway server. We are going to make it as a resource server. So what is the role of resource server? The resource server should not respond to the clients who are sending the requests without a valid access token. So this way we are securing our gateway server so that only authenticated client applications. They can invoke our edge server. Once my edge server receives the access token, it is going to validate the same access token with the auth server. Behind the scenes it is going to check with the auth server saying that. Did you issue this access token? Is this a valid access token? Once the keyclock or the auth server confirms saying that this access token is valid back to the gateway server, the gateway server at the Edge server is going to forward the request that it received from the client application to the individual microservices like accounts, loans and cards microservice. So here you may have a very good question, which is we are not protecting our individual microservices like accounts, loans and cards. So what is stopping my external client application to directly invoke accounts microservice, loans microservice and cards microservice. There can be some hacker or they can be some rogue client application. They can easily skip this process and they can directly try to communicate with the individual microservices. So that's a very good question. So let me try to answer the same. Whenever we are trying to deploy our accounts, microservice, our loans, our cards, microservice in the higher environments with the help of Docker compose or with the help of Kubernetes, we can make these microservices deployed behind a firewall or behind a Docker network so that any external client application they cannot communicate directly only the component which is deployed inside the same firewall or which is deployed inside the same Docker network can communicate with these services. So what is that component which we are going to deploy inside the same firewall or inside the same network? It is the gateway server and with that the client applications is not going to have any option except to forwarding the request to the gateway server. They can never be able to directly invoke the individual microservice. So I hope you are clear, but if you are thinking like why not make our individual microservices also to be as a resource server, then let me stop you there because it's a very valid assumption, but we should not make them resource server. The reason is if you make all your internal microservices also resource server, you will unnecessarily complicate things. And on top of that the performance of all your microservices will be impacted. Because whenever you make your individual microservices as a resource server, every time they are going to expect the access token for each and every request, even for the internal communication that is happening within your organization network. And whenever the access token is involved, the individual microservices they have to validate the same with the keyclock or the auth server. So this will unnecessarily bring a lot of complexities and performance issues. But I'm not saying we should not make our internal communication between the microservices to be completely unsecure. There are other, better industry standards and approaches which I will try to explain when we are trying to deploy our microservices into Kubernetes cluster. I hope you are clear with this discussion. Like I highlighted, we need to use these client credentials grant type flow only in the scenarios where two different backend applications or two different APIs are trying to communicate with each other. So there is an API which I hosted inside my gateway server. So some other API are some other backend service or some other external service application. They're trying to invoke my API. Since there is no end user involved, since there is no application involved, this is the perfect grant flow that we can use inside this scenario. I hope this is clear. Now let me try to explain the same steps in a more funny way. Like we know inside our drama we are going to have three actors, client, resource server and auth server. They're sitting idle, they're enjoying their holidays. Now to my client application someone gave a work saying that go ahead and invoke the APIs available inside the Easy Bank microservices network and get me some account details or get me some card details or loan details. With that the holidays for the client application completed. Now immediately client application will go ahead and ask the resource server saying that I'm trying to invoke so and so API, please give me the response. You can see inside this step there is no authentication, there is no access token. Seems this guy are this client is new to the oauth2 flow. The resource server at the Gateway server is going to respond. Sorry buddy, I can only process the request, who provides an access token from the auth server. So go ahead and get an access token from the auth server. Now my client application, which is a lazy application, again trying to complain within itself, saying that now I should send the request again to different server. Oh, seems a pretty bad day for me. So that's what it is thinking. As a next step, it has to send the request to the auth server saying that Hey auth server, please give me the access token and the auth server is going to respond. I can't give you access token just like that. I don't know who you are. In order to get an access token from me, you need to register with me and the same needs to be approved by my admin. So the client don't know all the story. So behind the scenes what it will do is it will take enough approvals and it will complete the registration process with the Keycloak server. Once this registration process is completed as a next step, the client application this time is going to invoke the auth server again saying that please give me an access token, this time I have a valid clientId, client secret. Please take this and give me an access token. So now my ad server will say Don't get too much excited. I'm going to show lot many access tokens to many clients. Seems you are pretty excited about this. This is very normal to me. So after saying this, the kick lock is going to validate the client credentials and it is going to respond back with the access token. If the key clock supports OpenID also in such scenarios, along with the access Token, the client also going to get the ID token as well. So in the step six, the ad server is going to say, Congratulations buddy, you got an access token here, your access token, all the best. With that, the client application feels very happy and it feels like it accomplished something. But the story not ended there. It has only just access token. It didn't get . It don't have actual resources staying inside the easy bank network. So soon it will realize this and it is going to forward the request to the resource server this time along with the request, it is also going to send the access token. Now as a next step, my resource server are my spring cloud Gateway is going to validate the same access token with the auth server. So the Spring Cloud Gateway says, I can't believe this client application. Last time also this guy came to me and asked me to send the response without any access token. This time, I'm not sure if this guy came with a valid access token or he's trying to bluff me with some random access token. The gateway gossiped all this into the Keycloak auth server. Now my keyclock auth server says, don't worry, I'm going to validate this for you. And once the validation is completed, the ad server confirm back to the gateway saying that, since this guy is valid, please go ahead and process his request and respond the same to him. So now my spring cloud gateway is going to forward the request to the individual microservices like accounts, cards and loans. And once the response is received from the individual microservices, it is going to forward the same back to the client application. And with this, the story ended happily. Now the client application received the response from the gateway and with that now everyone can go back to the holiday mode. I hope this is clear. Now, I know this is completely theory.
