39947558.title: Introduction to Kubernetes Service types
39947558.transcript: As of now, we deployed all our microservices with the service type as LoadBalancer. Previously I told you that on high level LoadBalancer will expose all your microservices to the outside world, like all the applications who are staying outside of your cluster also, they can access your microservices. The same type LoadBalancer we have mentioned in all the microservices like under loans also, we have mentioned the same for Cards, for Gateway, for Eureka, for Keycloak and for config server. In all the places we have mentioned these type as LoadBalancer. But do you think this is a correct approach to expose all our services to outside of the cluster? Off course not, because all the clients who are trying to communicate with our microservice, they should only communicate through the gateway server, which is going to act as an edge server inside our microservice network. So we should only make our gateway server as a load balancer and all the remaining microservices, we should not use the LoadBalancer. But before we try to do that, we need to understand what are the other options that we have to expose our microservices within Kubernetes cluster or to outside of the Kubernetes cluster. So let's try to understand the same inside this lecture. Like you can see here inside Kubernetes, these are the three mostly are majorly used service types and these services are cluster IP nodeport and load balancer. As of now, we are using only LoadBalancer. So every microservice developer should aware about these three service types at a bare minimum, because without knowing the service type, the microservice developer cannot really understand how to expose a microservice to the outside of the cluster or within the cluster. So that's why we are going to do some good amount of discussion around these three service types. So the very first service type that I want to discuss here is cluster IP service type. So if you don't mention the service type inside your Kubernetes manifest file, then this is that default service that Kubernetes is going to consider. The name itself is self-explanatory. Whenever we are using ClusterIP service, the Kubernetes cluster is going to create an internal IP address that can be used for the internal communication within the Kubernetes cluster. So whenever we don't want to expose our microservice to the outside world or to the outside of the Kubernetes cluster, then we should use the cluster IP service. So here in the slide I clearly highlighted someone trying to send the external traffic and that is strictly not allowed because for the outside people, whatever cluster IP that we are going to get, it is not going to make any sense to them. They will simply get not reachable or unknown IP address, whereas other microservices or other applications within your container. They can try to communicate with your microservice with the help of cluster IP. So let's try to take an example of accounts microservice itself. If you see here under the service.yaml manifest file, I have mentioned the service type as ClusterIP. Under ports I have mentioned the port as 80 and the target port as 8080. So whenever we mention the target port as 8080, that means my accounts microservice is going to start at the port 8080 inside my accounts related pod but it is going to be exposed to the other clients inside the Kubernetes cluster at the Port 80. So that's why if you see here, I have two pods of accounts because I have mentioned the replicas as two. And inside these two parts, I have two different accounts microservices are running and both of them are started at the port, 8080. Usually these pods will be deployed in two different worker nodes. Like here I have worker node one and worker node two. But if you see here, whoever want to communicate with my accounts microservice, they should use the ClusterIP that is generated by the Kubernetes cluster or they can use the service name that we have mentioned inside the service Kubernetes manifest file. Along with the service name at the ClusterIP, . they should also forward the request at the Port 80 because we exposed all our accounts microservice related containers to the other applications inside the Kubernetes cluster at the Port 80. If needed you can also mention the port value as 8080, which is same as your target port or your container port. So if you see the beauty of Kubernetes here, suppose think like some other application inside your Kubernetes cluster is trying to send the internal traffic at Port 80 using ClusterIP or using service name. In this scenario, the Kubernetes is going to do the load balancing behind the scenes it will decide to which pod the request has to send. And once this decision took by the Kubernetes cluster, it is going to forward the request to the corresponding pod. So my other application inside the Kubernetes cluster, who is trying to invoke the accounts microservice, they don't have to know what is the actual pod or what is the actual worker node where my accounts microservice is deployed, they will simply invoke with the help of ClusterIP or with the help of service name, which is accounts. So this NodePort service will be exposed on top of the ClusterIP service. Now since I have mentioned the service type as NodePort, my Kubernetes cluster is going to accept the external traffic at the NodePort value that we have defined, which is 32593. So whoever wants to send a traffic to my accounts microservice first they should know that, what are the IP addresses are worker nodes that we have inside our Kubernetes cluster. So let's assume one of the external client application. They want to consume my accounts microservice. So once the traffic is received at this node port, my Kubernetes cluster is going to forward that request to the cluster IP address at the service name that we have defined. And from the cluster IP, the load balancing will distribute between the two different parts available in two different worker nodes. But there is a drawback here with this NodePort service, my external client applications, they should know what is the IP addresses of worker node one or worker Node two. Suppose think like there is a scenario. One of the worker node has some issues, then Kubernetes automatically can kill that worker node. And in the place of that worker node, it is going to bring a new worker node. That means it is going to get a new IP address. So in this kind of scenarios, the client applications should always update their worker node details or IP address details. So that's why most of the people, they don't use this NodePort service. Instead they will go to the LoadBalancer service. So let's try to understand how this is different from the NodePort. So this is very similar to NodePort, but on top of the NodePorts that we have exposed, the Kubernetes is going to provide you a load balancer. And this LoadBalancer will have a public IP address, which is never going to change until unless a Kubernetes admin is going to change. So the same public address can be mapped to a DNS name or to your domain name and to this domain name or to this public address. The client applications, they can send the traffic. So whenever my LoadBalancer, which is staying outside my Kubernetes cluster, is receiving the traffic, it will identify to which worker node it has to send the request. Suppose think like it decided to send the request to the worker node one. In that scenario, the request will be forwarded to the NodePort and from the node port the traffic will go to the ClusterIP and from ClusterIP it will eventually go to the accounts microservice container. So what is the advantage that we have with this Load Balancer? We can expose the traffic to the external world just like inside NodePort, but compared to NodePort LoadBalancer service has an advantage, which is it is going to provide a LoadBalancer, which is always going to have a public IP address which you can map to your domain name regardless of how many worker nodes you are trying to create inside your Kubernetes cluster or how many worker nodes you are trying to delete them. So regardless of whatever is happening inside your Kubernetes cluster, it is not going to impact your client application in any way because the LoadBalancer is always going to be static with the IP address at the domain name that you have mapped. And the same can be used by your client applications. And this LoadBalancer, whatever created by the Kubernetes cluster, it is always going to track what is happening inside the Kubernetes cluster. Right now I have two replicas of accounts microservice deployed into two worker nodes. If I try to increase the replicas to three, it may deploy it into another worker node. So all those details will be tracked by this LoadBalancer and the request accordingly will be distributed to the multiple worker nodes. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39947732.title: Introduction to Helm & the problems that it solves
39947732.transcript: In the previous section, I gave a very quick introduction about Helm. Now, inside this lecture, let's try to understand more details about Helm. If someone asks you what is Helm, you can simply reply saying that Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes. The main objective of Helm is to help developers and DevOps team members to manage Kubernetes projects and deployments by offering a more efficient approach in handling the Kubernetes manifest files. Regardless of how many microservices you have inside your microservice network, Helm is going to make your life easy without helm like we discussed previously, we need to maintain lot many Kubernetes manifest files. Without helm like we discussed previously, we need to maintain all the Kubernetes manifest files like for deployment service config map for each and every microservice that you are going to deploy inside your microservice. And apart from maintaining these Kubernetes manifest files, your DevOps team members also they should manually apply or delete these Kubernetes manifest files with the help of Kubectl commands. But like I said, if we use Helm, we can overcome these challenges. And here you may have question like how Helm is going to help us to overcome these challenges. For the same helm is going to follow a packaging format called Charts. Inside Helm, a chart is a collection of files that describes a related set of Kubernetes resources. Suppose inside your microservice network, if you have 50 microservices, you can club all these 50 microservices related manifest files into a single component called Chart Inside Helm. So using the same single chart, we can deploy a simple application or any kind of complex application which can include Http servers, REST APIs, databases, supporting components like cache. So regardless of how complex your microservice network is, you can always try to create a chart specific to your organization. And with that, the maintenance of your Kubernetes deployments is going to be super easy. And one more beauty of this helm charts is, a chart can have a child charts or dependent charts as well, just like in Java, how we can have parent class or child class or dependent classes very similarly inside helm charts also, we can maintain the dependent charts and child charts in case if you have dependency on other third party components, you can try to add the helm charts of those components into your organization charts. This way you can install whole dependency tree of your project, including your own microservices and dependent components with just a single command. And like I said, Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes. And here you may have a question. What is a package manager? Package manager is a component that can help you in installing or uninstalling or upgrading your software packages. Just like how we have helm for Kubernetes, very similarly, we also have other famous package managers inside the industry. For example, Pip is a package manager that can help you to install any Python packages. And very similarly for JavaScript, we have a package manager with the name NPM. Using this NPM package manager, we can install any kind of JavaScript libraries like Angular, React. All these libraries we can easily install and set up inside our local system with the help of this NPM package manager. Very similarly, helm is the best way to find, share and use softwares built for Kubernetes. I hope this is clear. Now let me try to explain you very quickly on how Helm is going to solve all the challenges that we have discussed. Without Helm like we discussed, we need to maintain separate Kubernetes manifest files for all the microservices inside a project. For example, think like I have accounts, loans and cards, microservice. If I want to access them, either using external traffic or with the help of internal traffic, I should expose them with the help of Service object inside Kubernetes. So if you try to closely observe these manifest files, the skeleton of these files is almost same or static except a few dynamic values, like the very first element API version, which is V1 is going to be static for all the files. The same applies for kind object post that will be having metadata name inside the metadata name, we are going to have a dynamic value which is specific to this particular microservice. Post that and a specification selector. The app name is going to be the dynamic value and the type also is going to be a dynamic value based upon our requirements. We can use load balancer or cluster IP, but coming to the protocol, TCP is going to be a static value and the port and target port will have dynamic values which are specific to each microservice. So apart from these few dynamic values, the whole skeleton or template of the Kubernetes service manifest file is going to be the same. This is where Helm is going to come into picture and it will try to make our life easy. Now, let me quickly show you how Helm is going to approach this problem. So whenever we are using Helm, First, we are going to create a single template.yaml file for the Service object regardless of how many microservices you have inside your cluster, you're always going to have a single template.yaml file like you can see here. This template Yaml files will have static values along with the static syntax structure. If you closely observe this helm service template file at some places it is trying to accept the dynamic values. For example, under the metadata name it is going to accept the dynamic value with the help of values and inside the Values object, we need to pass a variable with the name deployment label. So whatever you mention inside this double curly braces is going to represent a dynamic value which we are going to inject at runtime whenever we are trying to deploy our microservices into Kubernetes cluster. For example, think like we have this helm service template file. As a next step, what I can do is, I can try to provide an values.yaml file which is specific to accounts microservice. So inside this values.yaml I'm going to maintain all the key and values you see inside my Values.yaml The very first property that I'm trying to create here is deployment label. Against that I'm mentioning a name which is accounts. The same value that we have defined inside the Values.yaml is going to be applied to the the service template file at runtime and behind the scenes helm is going to automatically create the account microservice specific service manifest file. Very similarly, you just have to provide the values.yaml for other microservices. With that, you will always have a single template file which is going to be maintained by the helm. But as a developer or as a DevOps team member, we should provide the Values.yaml for each microservice. And behind the scenes, using these values that we have defined. The helm is going to generate the Kubernetes manifest files at runtime for all your microservices. On top of that, we don't have to execute these Kubernetes manifest files manually. Whenever we are using Helm, we are going to achieve all the deployment of our microservice with a single command. So by following these approach, Helm is going to solve these problems. And if you ask me what are the problems that helm try to solve, The very first advantage, I would say as Helm is going to support packaging of your Kubernetes manifest files into a single helm chart, The same helm chart can be distributed into a public repository or a private repository you can share with others. So it's up to you how you want to leverage that helm chart. This is very similar to our Java code. We can store that in a centralized location if needed we can share our code with others. Very similarly, we are going to maintain the helm charts. That's why we are calling Helm as a package manager for Kubernetes. And the next problem that Helm solves is it is going to make developers or DevOps team members life easy because it is going to support easier installation. With the help of Helm, you can always deploy or upgrade or rollback or uninstall your entire microservice application into your Kubernetes cluster with a single command. No need to run any manual commands with the help of Kubectl, and at last Helm is going to support, release or version management as well, which means whenever needed, you can roll back your entire Kubernetes cluster to the previous working state with a single command. Whereas with Kubernetes manifest files like we discussed previously, we can only roll back a particular microservice. What if you want to roll back your entire Kubernetes cluster. In such scenarios, helm is going to help us. So we are going to discuss about all these features and we're also going to see the demo of all these features in the coming lectures. But for now, please note that Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes, which is very similar to other package managers like Pip and NPM. So with the help of these helm, we can make our life easy in terms of installing, rollback, upgrade or uninstalling of the microservices into the Kubernetes cluster. I hope you are clear with this quick introduction about the helm. I know you may have many questions about Helm. Don't worry. When we try to start discussing more about helm charts in the coming sections. It is going to make more sense to you. Thank you. And I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39947942.title: Introduction to Server-side service discovery and load balancing
39947942.section: Server-side service discovery and load balancing using Kubernetes
39947942.transcript: Long back when we are discussing about Eureka Server, we discussed about client side discovery and the load balancing that is going to happen at the client side. Inside this approach, all the microservices inside our microservice network, they are responsible for registering themselves with the service registry like Eureka during the startup. And similarly they should also unregister whenever they are shutting down. And apart from registering and unregistering my microservices, they should also send a regular heartbeat to the Eureka Server to prove their healthy status. In this kind of setup, our environment. Whenever a microservice wants to communicate with another microservice within your microservice network, then in such scenarios the microservice, which is trying to communicate with other microservice, it has to first connect with the Eureka Server to get the all the running instances of other microservice. Think like my accounts microservice want to communicate with the loans microservice. In this scenario my accounts microservice will ask the Eureka server what are the details of the loan service. How my Eureka server will know the details of the loans Microservice Because all the loans microservice instances during the startup, they will register themselves with the Eureka server along with their details like host name, port number and other instance details. So this is going to happen in the step one like you can see now in the step two where my accounts microservice is trying to ask Eureka Server like please give me the details of the loan service as a response to the step two inside the step three, my Eureka server is going to give you all the IP address or the instance details of the loans microservice and think like my Eureka server has given two IP address details where my loans microservice is available. So with these two instance details of loans microservice, my accounts microservice is going to have a problem. The problem is it has to decide which instance, it has to forward the request. So that's why it is going to do the load balancing with the help of spring cloud load balancer and after the process of load balancing, it is going to choose one of the instance details and to the same the request is going to be sent in the step four. So inside this approach you can see the client microservice or the accounts microservice is itself is responsible for the load balancing. That's why we call this approach as client side load balancing and client side service discovery. We can follow this approach, but this approach has some disadvantages. The disadvantage is the developers they have to manually maintain the Eureka server. They have to create a spring boot application. They have to convert that as an Eureka server post that they should also make changes in all the microservices to connect with the Eureka Server, to register their details with the Eureka Server. So they have to make some good amount of configurations. So this extra burden on the developers can be avoided if we follow the server side service discovery and server side load balancing. But this approach has an advantage which is with the help of client side load balancing the client application is going to have a complete control on the load balancing. Inside Spring Cloud Load Balancer, there are many strategies that the client applications can follow to perform the load balancing, whereas if you go with the server side service discovery and server side load balancing, you are not going to have any control on the load balancing. But the server side discovery and load balancing is going to provide an advantage, which is you don't have to make any changes inside your microservices. They don't have to register themselves with the service discovery like Eureka Server. So let's try to understand more details about the server side service discovery and the load balancing. We can follow the server side service discovery and load balancing only if we are using Kubernetes cluster for our microservices deployment. If you are not deploying your microservices into Kubernetes, then you cannot use this approach. Inside this approach, there will be a service discovery inside your Kubernetes cluster, which is responsible to monitor all the application instances and maintaining the details of them. Like you can see here in the step one, my Kubernetes discovery server, unlike Eureka, this server does not require the applications to explicitly register with the server. Instead, the Discovery server itself uses the Kubernetes APIs to fetch details about the Kubernetes services and endpoints of all the microservices. So inside this scenario I have given loans example. So my Kubernetes Discovery server is going to query Kubernetes API to fetch all the details of the loans microservice instances. Now coming to the client side story like think like the accounts microservice want to communicate with the loans microservice in this scenario. So whenever we are following service side discovery and load balancing the client application at the accounts microservice, they don't have to connect with any discovery server. They can simply send the request to the Kubernetes service using the service name at the host name or DNS name exposed by the Kubernetes. Behind the scenes, this Kubernetes service, which you have created with the help of cluster or load balancer service types, it is going to work with the Discovery server. Both the Discovery Server and Kubernetes service, they are going to decide on the load balancing and choose one of the instances of loans microservice to which the request from the accounts microservice is going to be forwarded. So here it is very clear that the load balancing is going to happen at the server side, which is inside the Kubernetes cluster and the client application at the client service, which is accounts microservice, It does not bother about any load balancing and it don't have to register or it don't have to connect with any discovery server like we followed in the Eureka Server approach. So this approach has some advantages and disadvantages. The advantage is the developers are freed from all the maintenance of Eureka Server manually and at the same time making configuration changes or establishing connection between all the microservices with the Eureka Server. So all those changes the developer don't have to do because right now the responsibility will be with the Discovery server to fetch the details of the running microservices instances. So that is one of the advantages that we have coming to the disadvantage the only disadvantage with this approach is, the clients application are the developers will not have any control on the load balancing. We as a developers are the client microservice. We can simply forward the request to the service URL and the load balancing strategy or algorithm is completely decided by the Kubernetes cluster, so I hope you are clear about the server side service discovery and load balancing. Inside this section, I'm going to show you how to establish server side service discovery and load balancer inside our Microservice network. In the same process, we can get rid of Eureka Server inside our microservice network. So there are no good and bad approaches. So you can choose one of them based upon your own business requirements. But it's my responsibility to show you both approaches. The first approach, which is client side service, discovery and load balancing. We already discussed during the discussion of Eureka Server. Now, since we have good understanding around Kubernetes cluster, let's try to implement this approach also so that you will be clear about server side service, discovery and load balancing. I hope you are clear about the agenda of this section. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39948066.title: Benefits of Kubernetes Ingress & the kind of traffic it handles
39948066.section: Introduction to Kubernetes Ingress, Service Mesh (Istio) & mTLS
39948066.transcript: Inside this lecture, let's try to understand why should we use Ingress? What are the benefits provided by the ingress to any organizations who are using microservices? The very first advantage of ingress is it is going to act as a single entry point into your Kubernetes cluster, which means it is going to act as an edge server using which you can configure a single entry point for multiple microservices you have inside your Kubernetes cluster. And this is going to make our life easy to manage external access to our microservices. The same advantage provided by the Spring Cloud Gateway as well. But like I said before, there is no good or bad approach between Spring Cloud Gateway and Kubernetes ingress approach. It all depends upon the team structure and the team technical skills of an organization. And apart from the single entry point, Kubernetes Ingress is also capable of TLS/SSL termination. We all know inside the web we always communicate with the help of secured protocols like TLS/SSL. That's why inside the browsers you will always see the Https protocol being used. Why do we use this TLS/SSL approach? When our data traveled from client location to the server, we need to make sure that no one is stealing our data. If you send the data using plain text, any hacker can intercept your request and he can steal the data. Whereas when we use Https protocol with the help of TLS, all your data is going to be traveled using an encrypted format. And this encrypted format cannot be decrypted by the hacker because only the servers know how to decrypt it. But these Https are TLS communication comes with the cost of performance. That's why it is not going to be a good idea if you want to follow the same https communication even within your cluster because no hacker can enter into your Kubernetes cluster other than using edge server. That's why there is no meaning of forwarding the same https traffic to the inside of the Kubernetes cluster as well. So that is going to bring some serious performance impacts. That's why many organizations, they don't want to carry that TLS communication into the Kubernetes cluster. So how they are going to achieve by terminating the TLS communication, that means at the ingress layer or at the edge server, they are going to convert that Https protocol to the Http. So once the data is entered into your Kubernetes cluster, the protocol is going to be changed from the Https to the Http. We can achieve this also with the help of Ingress. So this is one of the advantage of ingress. The next advantage is like we discussed, it is going to help you to implement path based routing and at the same time host based routing. We can define path based routing like if someone is trying to access my domain with the path /app1 then the request has to go to one of the service. And similarly app2 has to go to other service. Whereas sometimes organizations may use host based routing. That means they are going to have subdomains like app1.example.com or app2.example.com. So in these kind of scenarios they can define host based routing like someone is trying to access so-and-so subdomain. Then please forward the request to so-and-so service. And apart from routing the request, the ingress is also capable of load balancing the request and distributing the traffic among multiple parts of same service. So how it is going to do with the help of cluster IP service behind the scenes, the ingress is going to forward the request to the cluster IP and the cluster IP service is going to forward the request to one of the pod where the container is deployed. And apart from these advantages, we also have a concept called annotations inside Ingress. Using these annotations, we can configure additional settings such as rewriting rules, custom headers or adding authentication and authorization. So all these kind of additional capabilities can be achieved with the help of annotations. So these are all the advantages of Kubernetes Ingress. But like I said, we don't have to learn everything about Kubernetes Ingress because it is a responsibility of the Kubernetes developer to set up an ingress. I'm just trying to share this information for you so that you are ready for any kind of scenario. Some organizations may say we're going to set up Kubernetes Ingress. You don't need to worry as a developer about the Edge server. So in such scenarios, you should know what is Kubernetes ingress. Otherwise you are going to scratch your head. So to avoid that scenario, I'm trying to share this information. So whatever ingress is capable, our spring cloud gateway is also capable, like it is capable of path based routing, host based routing, single entry Point, load balancing. It is capable of authentication and writing cross-cutting concerns, and it is also capable of TLS/SSL termination. So everything is possible with the help of Spring Cloud Gateway as well. But at the end of the day, we need to go with the option choose to by the project architect or the project leadership. So if they ask us to go with the Spring Cloud Gateway, we need to go with that. Otherwise, if they select the Kubernetes ingress, then we need to go with this approach. I hope this is clear. As a next step, let me highlight you one more time. What is the difference between ingress controller and service type load balancer. Like we discussed before, both of them are capable of exposing your microservices to the outside world. But Ingress offers more advanced routing and traffic management capabilities compared to the load balancer. So if your organization is a very small organization where you have limited set of microservices and you are developing some low critical microservices, then you may go with the load balancer. You don't want a complex setup like ingress controller. Now, as a next step, let me try to introduce few jargons that you may hear whenever you are trying to involve in a microservice discussion. And these jargons are around the types of traffic handled by the ingress controller. So there are few jargons like Ingress traffic, Egress traffic and a North-South traffic. So what is ingress traffic? Any traffic that is entering a Kubernetes cluster, we call it, as a ingress traffic. And similarly, what is Egress traffic? It is opposite of ingress traffic. So any traffic which is exiting a Kubernetes cluster, we call it, as a Egress traffic. In other words, this ingress and egress traffic, we call it as a North-South traffic traffic entering and exiting a Kubernetes cluster. We can also call it as a North-South traffic. So your ingress or ingress controllers are capable of handling these traffics like North-South traffic or Ingress, Egress traffic. So here you may have a question like, okay, my ingress is capable of handling all the traffic that is entering and leaving my cluster. How about the traffic that is going to happen between your microservices inside Kubernetes cluster? So that's where we are going to discuss the next topic, which is service mesh. So let's try to understand more details about service mesh inside the next lecture. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

39948070.title: Introduction to Service Mesh & it's capabilities

So you will hear a lot of jargons as a microservice developer like north-south traffic, east-west traffic. So in this scenario, my accounts microservice trying to start a communication with the loans microservice That's why it asks the loans microservice to prove its identity. But think like in some other scenario the loans microservice is going to act as a client by sending the request to the accounts microservice. In such scenarios, definitely my loans microservice is going to ask the accounts microservice to prove its identity. Since this is going to happen at the both sides. We call this as mTLS, which is mutual TLS. I know this is a very complex to understand, but I'm assuming you are clear with all the detailed discussion that we have done so far. So your service mesh is capable of establishing these mTLS inside your microservices deployed within a Kubernetes cluster. The service mesh is capable of issuing certificates if needed you can also configure how soon the certificates can expire and how soon the certificates has to be renewed. So all such configurations we can do with the help of service mesh. But we as a developer don't have to know how to implement these mTLS inside the microservice communication with the help of service mesh. We should only be aware about this concept. I hope this is clear before we try to close this lecture, let's try to quickly understand why we should use mTLS, what are the advantages that it provides whenever we implement these inside a microservice network. The very first advantage is you're going to have mutual authentication. That means both the client and server, they are going to make sure they both verify themselves. And when they follow mutual authentication, then definitely the next advantage that we have is protection against impersonation, which means no other microservice or no other component inside your Kubernetes cluster. They cannot steal your data by acting themselves as a lone microservice or any other microservice with whom you are trying to communicate. Because they should have their valid digital certificate to prove themselves inside the microservice network. And the next advantage that we have here is granular access control. With the help of mTLS, we can enforce fine grained access control, allowing organizations to specify which microservices are permitted to communicate with which microservices. Moving on to the next advantage. With the help of mTLS, we are going to have resistance to credential compromise. We all know the basic approach of establishing communication between two applications is by using some service accounts, just like how we humans have username and password, we can give some service account details to each application. But for some reason, if those credentials are compromised, still mTLS is going to act as a second layer because without digital certificate the communication will never establish. And with that we can easily avoid credential theft or brute force attacks. The next advantage that we have with the mTLS is, simplified key management. Because inside mTLS the trusted certified authority is going to be your service mesh only. So issuing a new certificate or renewing an existing certificate is going to be super easy and does not involve any money. Because you're not using any third party certificate authority. And using these mTLS, we can also issue certificates to any number of microservices. That means there won't be any scalability issues regardless of how many microservices you have. You can issue the new certificates if needed, you can rotate the existing certificates. So especially these mTLS is going to simplify the operations inside large scale deployments. And whenever an organization uses mTLS, it is going to be compliant with the many compliances like GDPR, HIPAA, DSS. So these are all the industry standards that every organization has to follow to make sure that they are securing the sensitive data and providing privacy between the applications communication. And at last, when we use mTLS, we are going to follow zero trust security framework. That means without a proper digital certificate, you're not going to establish a communication with other components, even if they are present inside your own organization network. I'm assuming your super, super clear about mTLS and you have some valuable information. So as an advanced microservice developer, you should aware about all these advanced topics. Having a theory knowledge about them is good enough. You don't have to be expert because someone who has years of experience around setting up service mesh, they can only perform this job. I hope this is all clear to you. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture bye.

46134615.title: Shared Libraries in Microservices - Part 1
46134615.transcript: Many times microservice developers, they will end up writing a lot of duplicate code in multiple microservices. Let's try to understand how to handle these code duplication challenge. Inside my microservices there is a DTO class with the name ErrorResponseDto. This Dto class has exactly same code which is duplicated across all my microservices like accounts, cards, and loans. Off course I don't require any DTO inside my Eureka server, config server and gateway server. If you ignore these supporting services, I end up writing these ErrorResponseDto inside all the microservices. In future if I create more number of microservices, definitely I will need an ErrorResponseDto in the new microservice as well. In this kind of scenarios, you will be tempted to create a common library and move all these duplicate code into the common library and the same common library. You will try to import inside your individual microservices. This can be your basic approach, but there are better ways on how we can handle this. Let's try to understand the same. Whenever you are looking to build a shared libraries in microservices, you will end up with multiple approaches. Let's discuss one by one and let's try to identify which is a valid approach. The very first approach that any microservice developer can think is, to create a shared maven project containing all the common dependencies, or all the common code. This common code can be related to your utility classes or configurations, or logging security. It can be related to any functionality as long as it is common or being duplicated in multiple microservices. You may be tempted to create a single shared Maven project, but with this approach, there are some disadvantages. Let's try to understand what are those? Think like you are trying to build a common library inside these common library. You are trying to build some utility logic followed by security, followed by logging. If you keep adding these common related logic into your same library, the library is going to become a big fat jar. And with that, what is going to happen if some service, if they need only the utility logic, but not the security and logging related logic, the service still need to carry all these unused logic or these big fat jar inside its Docker image. So that's why this is definitely not a best practice or the recommended approach. With that, let's go to the second approach. The second approach that you may think here is, to split the shared code into the multiple smaller libraries. Inside this approach, what you are going to do is, you're going to create multiple smaller libraries handling the utility, security similarly logging. So since these are individual Maven libraries or Maven projects, the microservices, they have an option to select whatever common code that they want. So if you consider this as a microservice, if this microservice it only need the utility logic, then it can try to refer to the utility Maven project only, and it can safely ignore the security and logging related Maven dependencies. But there is a problem here. Usually inside the microservices projects. With this kind of setup, you will end up creating more than 20 or 30 different Maven projects or Maven libraries. Whenever you want to make some changes inside these individual Maven projects, then there will be some challenges in pushing the code or rising the pull request to your leads are maintaining the versioning numbers for all these maven dependencies. That's why this approach is also not feasible due to the challenges around the pull and push request and publishing multiple versions, which is going to complicate the entire management process. So with that, this approach is also not recommended. Now let's move on to the best and recommended approach, which is Multi-module project. In this approach, what is going to happen is anywhere you're going to create a bom inside your microservice inside the same bom, you can create Multi-module Maven project, where each module is going to focus a particular common functionality like logging, security, auditing, or any other functionality. So all these Multi-module projects, they are going to share a common parent bom, which is usually is going to be a bomb file. With this approach, what is going to happen is, all the challenges that we have discussed as part of approach to they will be resolved because we are maintaining all the common libraries as the submodules are as multi modules under a single parent palm. So they will be present inside the same GitHub repo. So the versioning maintaining of all these libraries, it is also going to be super simple. I'll try to show you the demo around these shared libraries in the next lecture. But before I try to close this lecture I want to provide a disclaimer around the shared libraries. Usually sharing code or sharing libraries between microservices is a debated topic. You will see few developers against these, whereas other developers supporting this approach. There is no single answer on how we can share the libraries in microservices. You as a developer, you need to make sure whether it is really worth to move my duplicate code into a shared library. If you have a common code or duplicate code, which is not going to bring any coupling related challenges or deployment related challenges inside your microservices, then you can happily use the shared library approach. But if you have a duplicate logic or common code, which is going to bring some tight coupling between the microservices or which is going to make the deployment process a complex one, then in such scenarios, you need to think about whether it is really worth building a shared library. If you are happy by maintaining some duplicate code in multiple microservices, then you can ignore the shared library. If you are going with the shared libraries approach. If it is bringing some challenges, then there is no worth of implementing the shared libraries in microservices. According to me, you should continue maintaining the duplicate code in all the microservices. I hope you are clear. Let's see the demo of this third approach in the next lecture. Thank you, bye.

46135223.title: Strangler Fig pattern
46135223.section: How do we right size our microservices & identify boundaries
46135223.transcript: When someone is looking to migrate their legacy or monolithic application into the latest microservices architecture, then there is a design pattern that people can follow. This design pattern is Strangler Fig pattern. So let's try to understand what is this pattern and how it is going to help the developers and organizations to migrate into the microservices architecture. Strangler fig pattern is a software migration pattern used to gradually replace or refactor a legacy system with a new system. In this pattern, what is going to happen ? The legacy application it is going to be replaced with a modern approach piece by piece, without disrupting the existing functionality. And here you might be wondering how this design pattern got this name, which is Strangler fig pattern. There is a very interesting story behind that. This pattern gets its name from the way a strangler fig plant grows around an existing tree by slowly replacing until the original tree is no longer needed. You can also see here I have mentioned the various stages of a strangler fig plant. This is how the strangler fig plant is going to look like. If you see just around the main tree. A new plant is trying to grow. When it grows fully, the original tree or the main tree, it is going to be completely replaced. Here in the very first stage you can see this is the original tree. To this original tree, a small plant started to grow on the sides and this small plant, it started growing onto the main tree itself. The new tree, it is trying to gradually grow and eventually only the new tree is going to be present. And the old tree, it is going to die or it is going to be completely replaced. The same strategy is organizations they are going to follow whenever they are trying to migrate their the legacy application into microservices. Usually following this pattern is strongly recommended if you are trying to modernize a large or complex legacy system. If your legacy system is very small, then there is no need to follow these strangler fig pattern only for the complex or large legacy systems. Only this pattern is recommended. You can also follow this pattern when you want to avoid the risk associated with a complete system rewrite or a big bang migration. What is a Big Bang migration? Suddenly, on a single day, you are going to migrate from legacy system to the new system. This kind of migration system will call them as Big Bang migration. There are certain risks if you try to follow the Big Bang migration by rewriting the entire legacy system. To avoid such risk, you can follow the Strangler fig pattern at last you can also consider this pattern when the legacy system needs to remain operational during the transition to the new system. I hope you got some gist of these strangler fig pattern. Let me show you an example so that it is going to be clear for you. As you can see here, initially there was a monolithic app. Just imagine this is a bank application, inside this bank application it supports functionalities around the cards, accounts and loans. Entire functionality is present inside these monolithic app. So what the development team they are going to do whenever they are following the strangler fig pattern is, first they are going to decide how to rightsize their microservice by following that domain driven design approach. As a next step, the legacy monolithic is carefully broken down into distinct domains, and services are gradually rewritten using newer technologies. As you can see here, the development team first, they have created an accounts microservice and the remaining functionality is still present inside the monolithic app. The remaining functionality is related to the cards and loans microservice. Once they feel everything around the accounts microservice is properly working in production. What they are going to do is, they are going to migrate the next component into the microservice. The next component is, cards here, once they satisfied with the deployment of cards and accounts microservice into the microservices environment, if they are happy with the results and the progress that they made, eventually they will try to migrate their loans as well. And towards the end stage you can see there is no monolithic app here. Because it is completely replaced with this approach developers team, they are going to get lot of benefits. The very first benefit is there is, very minimal risk associated with these migration. Since the development team, they are trying to migrate only a piece of component into microservices. If there are some issues, they should be easily handled and whatever learnings they have learned during the first migration, those learnings will be used while they are migrating other components. And the next advantage is this pattern chain allow the development team to migrate incrementally instead of going for the Big Bang migration. With the Big Bang migration, we'll have a lot of challenges. You can't roll back easily. And if there are any surprises, it's very hard to handle them for the development team. Whereas with the incremental migration, the development team will have enough opportunities to learn from their mistakes, to handle the issues and surprises. And the last advantage that developers they're going to get when they're following this pattern is, this pattern is going to help in performing testing and validations with greater efficiency. Since monolithic application is also going to be present inside this migration, they can always try to route the traffic between the microservices and legacy application. They can test by routing the traffic between the two components. If there are any issues, they can compare the results between the legacy and the microservices. For some reason, if the microservices is not working as expected, then they can try to navigate the 100% of the traffic to the monolithic application. Here, though, I'm trying to decrease the size of the monolithic app, but in reality, the accounts code and the cards code, it is going to still be present inside the monolithic app, but it will be in the dead state because there is no traffic is coming. But development team, like I said, they have flexibility. They can redirect 50-50% of the traffic between these two components. Once they are confident that microservices is working, they can send 100% of the traffic towards a microservice. Otherwise, they can roll back the traffic back to the monolithic application. Whenever someone is following this pattern, there are four different steps that they are going to follow. The very first one is Identification. As part of this identification process, they are going to identify how many microservices they need to do. Basically, they are going to perform the right sizing of the microservices by using the domain driven approach. The next stage is going to be the transformation where they are going to transform the components inside the legacy into the microservices. Once the transformation is successful. As you can see, both monolithic and microservices, they are going to be coexist. There will be a strangler facade introduced with the help of the strangler facade. The traffic between the legacy and the microservices can be handled. Usually this can be handled with the help of a API gateway. After the coexistence phase, at last, the elimination phase comes where they are going to completely eliminate the legacy application and the entire traffic will go to the microservices or the modern application. I hope you are clear with this pattern. Please make sure you are clear about this pattern because there is a very good chance inside the interviews, people may ask you around the migration. They can simply ask, how are you migrating your legacy into the microservice based application? The answer is very simple. You need to explain these Strangler fig pattern. Thank you and I'll catch you in the next lecture, bye.

46135647.title: Design patterns around API Gateway
46135647.section: Gateway, Routing & Cross cutting concerns in Microservices
46135647.transcript: Instructor: As of now, we have implemented an entry point or an edge server with the help of Spring Cloud Gateway inside our microservices setup. Around the same concept, we have some good amount of design patterns available in the industry. Inside this lecture, what I'm going to do is I'll try to explain all these design patterns around the API Gateway concept. In the same process, you will hear some jargons very first time. I want you to be aware about all these design patterns since they're going to be helpful in any microservice specific related interviews. The very first pattern that I want to explain here is API Gateway pattern. Whenever someone is trying to talk about this API Gateway pattern, they are referring to a component which is going to act as an entry point into your microservices. We build the same component with the help of Spring Cloud Gateway, so whenever you have this kind of setup, this entry point is going to take care of streamlining the communication security and routing to the microservices. As you can see here, we have client apps like web and mobile. Whenever a client is trying to send a traffic towards my microservice, this central component which is acting as an API Gateway, it is going to take care of various crosscutting concerns like security routing, communication. I hope you're clear with this pattern. This pattern is nothing but building a edge server or an unified entry point for your microservices. We have already done this inside this section with the help of Spring Cloud Gateway. The next pattern that we have is Gateway routing pattern, whatever edgeware or entry point component that you have built, if it is capable of routing the incoming client request to the appropriate backend microservices based upon various factors like URL, headers, and request parameters, then that edge server, we can assume it is implementing the Gateway routing pattern. Whatever edge server that we have implemented with the help of Spring Cloud Gateway, it is capable of performing the routing the request based upon the URL values. That's why we can safely assume we have implemented this pattern as well inside our microservices. The next pattern is Gateway offloading pattern. Don't get confused with all these jargons and with all these names. Once we are done with this lecture, I will request you to look at the slides one more time so that it is going to be clear for you. In this pattern, what is going to happen is if you are edge server or your entry point into the microservices, if it is capable of handling the crosscutting concerns such as security, caching, rate limiting, monitoring, then search component, we can assume it is implementing the Gateway offloading pattern. Since these edge server, it is offloading the cross-cutting concerns from the individual microservices, this name came, which is Gateway offloading pattern. So what kind of tasks that we can offload? All cross-cutting concerns like security, caching, auditing, load balancing, SSL termination, this kind of tasks we can safely offload to the Gateway. We can't offload the business logic of accounts to the Gateway because that will defeat the whole purpose of developing the microservices. Only the cross-cutting concerns we will be offloading, so whatever edge server that we have built so far with the help of Spring Cloud Gateway, since it is taking care of some crosscutting concerns, we can assume our Spring Cloud Gateway, it is implementing this pattern. In the coming sections, we are also going to implement security and other crosscutting concerns inside our edge server. I hope you are clear with this pattern. The next pattern that we have is Backend For Frontend pattern. The short name for this pattern is BFF. Whenever someone is following this pattern, what they're going to do is they're going to have multiple edge servers or entry points into their microservices network. Each of the edge server will be developed focusing a particular type of client. For example, here for my microservices, I'm expecting the traffic from various kinds of clients like web, mobile, and tablet. So for these different kind of clients, if I have different edge servers or API Gateways, then search implementation, we can call it as Backend Frontend pattern. So whenever a web application is trying to send a request to the microservice, they should always send the traffic to this API Gateway, which focus on the web. The same applies for mobile and tab. Here you may have a question, which is why should someone build various edge servers focusing various clients? This can be a question. Let's try to answer the same. Usually for complex applications or for big enterprise applications, they may have various requirements. For example, when they're trying to send the response to the mobile application or tablet, they may want to compress the response so that the compressed data can be easily processed by these lightweight clients like mobile and tablet. Similarly, they may have requirements to send various different types of responses based upon the client type. For example, for an API, if the request is coming from the web, they may want to send various details inside the response like images, transaction details, because usually web applications will have more space to display all this content. But coming to the mobile application, since the space is very limited, the response should have some limited information. Maybe they don't want to send the images, they only want to send the transactions information, and along with the transactions information, they want to send some summary information as well. These kind of client specific requirements, we should be able to easily implement with the help of Backend For Frontend pattern. So these microservices, they're always going to send the same response, but in between these edge servers, they're going to do some filtering on the response based upon these client applications. I hope you're clear about this pattern. Let's go to the next pattern. Many times we may have a requirement to fetch the information from multiple microservices and send this combined response to the client application. The client application may want to display or may want to process this combined data from various microservices. In this example, let's imagine the bank website has an UI page where they want to display the summary of account, summary of loans, and summary of cards. In the same page, they want to display the data from all these three microservices. In these kind of scenarios, there are multiple approaches that clients can follow. The very first and basic approaches, the client can individually call the accounts, loans and cards. With these, what is going to happen? Three different requests will go to the microservices, which is not the recommended approach because you are sending too much traffic. And the second approach is, which we already implemented in the previous lectures when we are discussing about the Eureka, in this approach, what we can do is we can send the request to one of the microservice, which is accounts. This accounts microservice will in turn make the call to the loans and cards, and inside the accounts microservice, the entire data is going to be combined and the single response will be sent back to the client application. The similar kind of setup we can do inside the edge server or inside the API Gateway as well. In this approach, what is going to happen is the API Gateway is going to take care of invoking all the multiple microservices and combining the data into a single response. So all this responsibility will be handled by the API Gateway. What API Gateway is going to do in this scenario, once the client send the request, my API Gateway is going to take the responsibility of invoking all the applicable microservices. The data is going to be combined here. The combined response will be sent back to the client application. Whenever you have implemented this kind of logic inside your API Gateway, you can assume you have implemented or followed the Gateway aggregator or Gateway composition pattern. So here what is happening, your Gateway is aggregating that data from all the microservices or it is composing all the data from the microservices. From the client perspective, they always going to make a single invocation to the API Gateway. Basically, inside the API Gateway, you're going to have some API with the name like fetch summary. From this fetch summary business logic, you can invoke all these individual microservices, combine the data and the combined data will be sent back to the client application. I hope you're clear with all these patterns. In total, I have discussed five different patterns which are related to the API Gateway. So please try to revise the slides that I have provided and make sure you are clear about these patterns. In future, whenever someone is asking about these patterns or jargons, don't get confused. Take a deep breath and try to answer them based upon the knowledge that you have acquired inside this lecture. Thank you, bye.
